coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dots/carbon nitride heterojunction with enhanced visible-light driven photocatalysis of nitric oxide: An experimental and DFT study
coredata.dc:creator: Cui, Yunpei
Huang, Xiaoxiang
Wang, Teng
Jia, Liuhu
Nie, Qianqian
Tan, Zhongchao
Yu, Hesheng
coredata.dc:description:
Visible light-driven photocatalysis for the degradation of nitric oxide (NO) has attracted considerable attention. Graphene quantum dots/graphite phase carbon nitride (GQDs/CN) heterojunctions were proposed as efficient visible-light responsive photocatalysts for NO treatment under the guidance of density function theory (DFT) simulation. Then, the GQDs/CN was synthesized via hydrothermal combination of GQDs and g-C3N4 (CN) produced from pyrolysis of citric acid (CA) and melamine, respectively. Afterwards, the GQDs/CN composite samples were characterized using XRD, XPS, FE-SEM, TEM, HR-TEM, BET, UV–Vis DRS, PL, photocurrent tests, and EIS. Later, the photocatalytic activity of the prepared GQDs/CN composites was evaluated by degrading NO at ppm level under visible light irradiation at conditions of 20 ppm of NO, 5% of O2, and 50% of relative humidity. Results show that 9GQDs/CN outperforms its counterparts. Compared to CN, the 9GQDs/CN increases the NO conversion rate from 61% to 90%, the selectivity of nitrate formation from 53% to 74%, and the value of DeNO
x index from −0.25 to 0.19. The enhanced performance results from the inclusion of GQDs, which increases the specific surface area, promotes the absorption of light, boosts the separation of photogenerated electrons and holes, and inhibits their recombination. Furthermore, we used active species detection experiment, IC, in-situ DRIFTS, ESR measurement, XPS VB, Mott-Schottky, and DFT calculations to systematically explore the mechanism behind the degradation of NO using GQDs/CN, and confirmed the formation of Type-Ⅱ heterojunction between GQDs and g-C3N4. In addition, 9GQDs/CN maintains a high NO conversion rate after 5 cycles of experiments, indicating its potential in industrial applications.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Visible light-driven photocatalysis for the degradation of nitric oxide (NO) has attracted considerable attention. Graphene quantum dots/graphite phase carbon nitride (GQDs/CN) heterojunctions were proposed as efficient visible-light responsive photocatalysts for NO treatment under the guidance of density function theory (DFT) simulation. Then, the GQDs/CN was synthesized via hydrothermal combination of GQDs and g-C3N4 (CN) produced from pyrolysis of citric acid (CA) and melamine, respectively. Afterwards, the GQDs/CN composite samples were characterized using XRD, XPS, FE-SEM, TEM, HR-TEM, BET, UV–Vis DRS, PL, photocurrent tests, and EIS. Later, the photocatalytic activity of the prepared GQDs/CN composites was evaluated by degrading NO at ppm level under visible light irradiation at conditions of 20 ppm of NO, 5% of O2, and 50% of relative humidity. Results show that 9GQDs/CN outperforms its counterparts. Compared to CN, the 9GQDs/CN increases the NO conversion rate from 61% to 90%, the selectivity of nitrate formation from 53% to 74%, and the value of DeNO
x index from −0.25 to 0.19. The enhanced performance results from the inclusion of GQDs, which increases the specific surface area, promotes the absorption of light, boosts the separation of photogenerated electrons and holes, and inhibits their recombination. Furthermore, we used active species detection experiment, IC, in-situ DRIFTS, ESR measurement, XPS VB, Mott-Schottky, and DFT calculations to systematically explore the mechanism behind the degradation of NO using GQDs/CN, and confirmed the formation of Type-Ⅱ heterojunction between GQDs and g-C3N4. In addition, 9GQDs/CN maintains a high NO conversion rate after 5 cycles of experiments, indicating its potential in industrial applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Nitrogen oxides (NOx) are air pollutants [1,2] that cause severe environmental issues such as photochemical smog, ozone layer depletion, and acid rain. They also impair human health [3]. Furthermore, nitric oxide (NO), accounting for 90–95% of NOx is difficult to treat because of its low solubility [4]. Therefore, efficient control of NO is critical to the abatement of NOx emissions. Existing de-nitrification technologies, including traditional adsorption and selective catalytic reduction (SCR), cannot remove NO at low concentration in a cost-effective fashion [5]. On the contrary, photocatalysis is deemed efficient, economical, and environmentally-friendly for the removal of NO [6,7].

Many semiconductors have already been used for NO photocatalysis [8,9]. Among them, graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) is preferred because of its high stability, low cost, visible-light response, and surface flexibility for chemical modification [10,11]. Although pure g-C3N4 has a narrow band gap and responds to visible light, its application is limited to low photocatalytic activity owing to fast recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes. Therefore, earlier researchers used modification techniques including metal doping [12], non-metal doping [13], precious metal deposition [14], and semiconductor coupling [15] to improve photocatalytic performance of g-C3N4.

Currently, zero-dimensional (0D) graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have been tested for the synthesis of composite materials, because they possess non-zero band gap, have good dispersibility, and can provide abundant active sites [16]. For example, Xie et al. [17] loaded GQDs into tin oxide (SnO2), which greatly improved light absorption and promoted the separation of photogenerated electrons from holes within the catalyst. Lei et al. [18] also reported that GQDs functioned as an efficient electron acceptor in CdS/GQDs nanocomposites. The GQDs increased the efficiency of photocatalytic H2 evolution. Liu et al. [19] constructed a Bi2O2CO3/GQDs Z-Scheme composite, which could efficiently degrade NO under visible light. In addition, our previous research [20] showed that introducing GQDs into Bi2WO6 to form a Z-Scheme heterojunction increased the NO conversion rate by 3.84 times.

Furthermore, the photocatalytic performance of g-C3N4 could be enhanced by combining it with 0D quantum dots (QDs). For instance, Liang et al. [21] synthesized MoS2 QD/g-C3N4 composites that enabled rapid spatial charge separation and improved photocatalytic H2 evolution. Jian et al. [22] also reported that the oxidizability, reducibility, and interfacial-charge-transfer capability of g-C3N4 were greatly improved by addition of F–TiO2 QDs. The literature shows that GQDs are expected to improve the photocatalytic capability of g-C3N4. To the best of our knowledge, the short-term exposure limit for NO is 25 ppm [23]. However, most earlier research focuses on the photocatalytic degradation of NO at ppb level. Furthermore, little information is available for the degradation of NO using GQDs/g-C3N4 (GQDs/CN). Thus, it is essential to study GQDs/CN photocatalytic degradation of NO at ppm level.

The reagents used in this study include melamine (C3H6N6, 99.0%), citric acid (CA, 99.8%), sodium hydroxide (NaOH, 96%), sodium sulfate (Na2SO4, 99.0%), potassium iodide (KI, 99.0%), potassium dichromate (K2Cr2O7, 99.8%), and anhydrous ethanol (99.7%). They were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (China), except for K2Cr2O7 from Xilong Scientific (China). They were used without further purification. In addition, a Direct-Q 5 UV System (Merck Millipore, Germany) was used to produce ultrapure water with a resistivity of 18.2 MΩ cm−1.

The preparation method of GQDs has been reported in our previous literature [20], and it is briefly presented as follows. First, 2 g of CA was loaded into a 25 mL round-bottomed flask and pyrolyzed at 200 °C for 30 min in an oil bath. Then, the pH value of the pyrolysis product, which was monitored using a pH meter (S470–K, METTLER TOLEDO), was adjusted to 7 using 0.25 M NaOH solution. The resultant solution was deemed stable and could be stored for several months at 4 °C for subsequent use.

The obtained solution containing GQDs was further purified for characterization. Specifically, the solution was stored in a 1500 Da dialysis bag for four days to produce pure GQDs. The dialysis water was changed every 5 h in the first two days and every half a day in the next two days. Half of the pure GQDs solution was freeze-dried for two days to obtain GQDs solids, and the other half was stored at 4 °C for subsequent PL test. After that, the GQDs solids were dispersed in 5 mL of absolute ethanol solution, followed by sonication for 20 min to obtain a uniform suspension for HR-TEM test.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of g-C3N4 and GQDs/CN nanomaterials
ce:section.ce:para: g-C3N4 was synthesized via thermal decomposition of melamine. First, 10 g of melamine was packed evenly in 10 mL alumina crucible with cover before calcination in a tube furnace at 550 °C for 4 h with a ramp of 5 °C∙min−1. Later, the obtained solid was ground thoroughly in an agate mortar for 15 min, and it is denoted as CN.

GQDs/CN composite was prepared by facile hydrothermal treatment. Fig. S1 in the Supporting Information (SI) shows the preparation process of GQDs/CN composite catalyst. Specifically, 1, 9, or 15 mL of GQDs solution was added into a beaker containing 0.4 g of CN. After magnetically stirring for 30 min, the precursor suspension was loaded into a 100-mL autoclave made of Teflon-lined stainless steel, which was heated at 180 °C for 4 h. After cooling the reactor to room temperature, the solid product was separated using a centrifuge running at 4500 rpm, followed by washing three times with ultrapure water and anhydrous ethanol. The resultant final samples were dried at 70 °C for 10 h. The samples corresponding to 1, 9, and 15 mL of GQDs solutions are referred to as 1GQDs/CN, 9GQDs/CN, and 15GQDs/CN, respectively. In addition, the weight ratio of GQDs added during preparation is acquired by weight difference method elaborated in Table S1. The mass ratio of GQDs solution in original prepared solution without purification can be calculated as 1.366 mg/mL. Hence, the weight ratios of GQDs in 1GQDs/CN, 9GQDs/CN, and 15GQDs/CN are 0.34 wt%, 2.98 wt%, and 4.87 wt%, respectively.

The photocatalytic activities of CN and GQDs/CN samples were evaluated in a continuous ISO reactor [24] which is 10 cm long, 5 cm wide, and 0.5 cm high. Fig. S2 shows the experimental setup for photocatalytic degradation of NO by visible light irradiation. First, 0.08 g of as-prepared photocatalysts were dispersed in 10 mL of ultrapure water before it was evenly coated onto a glass slide (5 × 10 cm2). Then, the coated plate was dried in an oven at 70 °C for 5 h. A simulated gas stream contained 20 ppm NO and 5% O2 at a relative humidity (RH) of 50%. The flow rates of NO (1001 ppm, N2 balance), N2 (99.999%), and O2 (99.999%) were set at 4 mL/min, 186 mL/min, and 10 mL/min by respective mass flow controllers (MFC, CS200A, Zhengzhou Ketan, China). The RH of the simulated gas stream was measured by a hygrometer (UNI-T UT333, China) with a repeatability of 0.1% of the measurement. Visible light source was provided by a 300 W Xe lamp (DP200A PN/2123XSL, China) equipped with a UV filter with a cut-off wavelength of 380 nm. The light source is vertically placed 15 cm above the quartz window on the upper side of the ISO reactor. The simulated gas passed over the catalyst in the reactor for 30 min to achieve the adsorption-desorption dynamic equilibrium before light irradiation. In addition, the concentrations of NO and NO2 at the outlet of the reactor were continuously monitored by a FTIR analyzer (MKS MultiGas 2060, USA) with a repeatability of 1% and an accuracy of 2% of the measurement.

Used catalysts were regenerated by thermal treatment for cyclic experiments to evaluate catalyst stability, which is important to industrial applications. In a typical regeneration process, the used catalysts were heated in an oven at 80 °C for 90 min, followed by natural cooling to room temperature, ready for next cycle. Each sample was tested for five cycles in total.

The solution sample for IC test was prepared in the following way. The used catalyst was scraped from the loading glass plate, and its weight was recorded (
m
I C
). The solid powder was then dispersed in 14 mL of ultrapure water and ultrasonicated for 20 min. After that, the suspension was filtered using a membrane with 0.45-μm micropores. The clear filtrate was used for the IC test to determine the concentration of N-containing anions formed in the photocatalysis.

The selectivity for the formation of NOx − (x = 2 or 3) quantifies the ability of a photocatalyst to convert NO into nitrite or nitrate ions or both. The major products of photocatalytic oxidation of NO are NO2 and NOx −. Because NO2 is more toxic than NO, it is preferred to achieve as high

According to American Conference of Governmental Industrial Hygienists (ACGIH), National Institute for Occupational Safety and Health (NIOSH), and Occupational Safety and Health Administration (OSHA), the short-term exposure limit for NO is 25 ppm while the value for NO2 is 1–3 ppm [23]. It makes NO2 8 to 25 times more dangerous than NO for short-term exposure. Then, Bloh et al. [25] conservatively assumed that the contribution of NO2 to NOx related air toxicity is 3 times that of NO, leading to the calculation of DeNOx index in Eq. (3). This indicator has also been accepted by Çağlayan et al. [23]; therefore, it is used in this work to quantify the environmental friendliness of a NO photocatalyst. The more positive the value of DeNO
x index, the more environmentally friendly the catalyst. The DeNO
x index is defined as Eq. (3):

K2Cr2O7 and KI were chosen as scavengers for electrons (e
− ) and holes (h
+ ) [26], respectively. In a typical test, 1.6 mmol of scavenger was dispersed in 10 mL of ultrapure water, which was ultrasonicated for 15 min to form a homogeneous solution. Then, 80 mg of catalyst was added into the scavenger solution, which was ultrasonicated for another 5 min. Finally, the solution was coated onto a glass plate and dried in an oven at 70 °C for subsequent use. The effects of
⋅
O 2 −
and
⋅ O H
radicals on the degradation of NO were clarified by removing O2 or H2O from the inlet gas stream to avoid the generation of
⋅
O 2 −
or
⋅ O H
in the photocatalytic process [27].

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) test, photocurrent response measurement, and Mott-Schottky (MS) analysis were performed on an electrochemical workstation (Model CHI660E, Shanghai Chenhua, China) with a standard three-electrode system. A standard saturated calomel electrode (SCE), Pt electrode, and indium-tin oxide (ITO) conductive glass coated with photocatalyst are reference electrode, counter electrode, and working electrode, respectively. The electrolyte was 0.2 M Na2SO4 solution.

The working electrodes were prepared following these steps. 8 mg of photocatalyst and 20 μL of Nafion solution (DuPont, China; 5 wt%) were dispersed in 1 mL of ethanol and ultrasonicated for 2 min to obtain a well-mixed suspension. Then, 200 μL of the suspension was deposited onto a quarter of the ITO conductive glass (1 × 4 cm2), followed by drying in the oven at 60 °C for 3 h.

EIS plots were obtained by A.C. Impedance (IMP) method at a bias voltage of 0.5 mV and a frequency in the range of 0.01–10,000 Hz in the dark. Photocurrent response curves were produced using the Amperometric i–t Curve method under the irradiation of a 300 W Xe lamp with a 380 nm UV cut filter. The MS curves were measured by Impedance-Potential (IMPE) method at a frequency of 1 kHz and a voltage of 5 mV with scanning potential from −1.2 V to 0.6 V in the dark.

In-situ DRIFTS tests were carried out using a Tensor II FT-IR spectrometer (Bruker) which was equipped with an in-situ diffuse-reflectance cell and reaction chamber. First, the samples were dried at 60 °C for 2 h before placing it into the reaction chamber. Second, the chamber was purged with N2 gas for 30 min, and the measured FT-IR spectra under this condition was used as the baseline. Third, simulated gas stream obtained by regulation flow rates of different gases (186 mL/min N2, 4 mL/min NO, and 10 mL/min O2) was fed into the chamber for adsorption for 30 min. Fourth, a 300 W Xe lamp (with a UV filter, λ > 380 nm) was used as light source to illuminate the samples, and the FT-IR spectra was collected every 10 min for 1 h. The infrared spectra were collected in the range of 800–2000 cm−1.

The Cambridge Sequential Total Energy Package (CASTEP) was used for density functional theory (DFT) simulation [28]. The exchange correlation function adopted the generalized gradient approximation (GGA) of Perdewe-Brukee-Ernzerhof (PBE). The energy cut-off was set to 500 eV, and the Monkhorst-Pack k-point was set to 3 × 3 × 1. The convergence criteria for forces acting on atoms, the maximum ion displacement, and the total stress tensor were set as 0.03 eV/Å, 0.001 Å, and 0.05 GPa, respectively. To simplify the calculation, a 2 × 2 × 1 g-C3N4 supercell was used to adsorb GQDs containing 16 C atoms, and the model vacuum layer was set 20 Å to avoid interaction between periodic structures. Spin polarization was used in all calculations, and the Grimme scheme was selected to correct the dispersion force of the structure. The adsorption energy was obtained using Eq. (4):

DFT calculations were performed to clarify the adsorption position and charge transfer between GQDs and g-C3N4. Six possible positions for the adsorption between GQDs and g-C3N4, as shown in Fig. S3, were predicted via the Monte Carlo method [29] and the simulated annealing procedure [30]. Table S2 tabulates their adsorption energies. Position 1 shown in Figs. S3a and S3b has the lowest adsorption energy of −2.79 eV, which indicates that g-C3N4 has the strongest adsorption interaction with GQDs at this position. Thus, Position 1 is selected for further calculations.

Fig. 1 and Fig. S4 present the charge density differences to illustrate the adsorption of GQDs on g-C3N4. Fig. S4a shows the top view of the optimized structure of GQDs/CN. The red area in Fig. 1a and b, and Fig. S4b indicate electron accumulation, and the blue area indicates the decrease of electron density. The color difference suggests that e
- gather near GQDs and that the electron density decreases around g-C3N4. Bader charge calculation by CASTEP shows that GQDs obtained 0.09 electrons, which supports that GQDs can be electrostatically adsorbed onto the g-C3N4. This electrostatic adsorption then leads to the formation of a local built-in electric field pointing from g-C3N4 towards GQDs. Upon light irradiation on GQDs/CN composite, photogenerated e
- excited from GQDs transport to g-C3N4 in the opposite direction of the built-in electric field, which is also proved by projected density of states (PDOS).

Comparison between Fig. 2 a and d shows that the valence band maximum (VBM) of the entire structure moves upwards after the adsorption of GQDs. Fig. 2c and f shows that the band gap of the system decreases after g-C3N4 adsorbs GQDs, which corresponds to the results of the UV–Vis test in Fig. 4a. The DFT results facilitate the exploration of mechanism of NO degradation using GQDs/CN (see Section 3.6).

PDOS of CN and GQDs/CN are calculated to clarify the coupling relationship between GQDs and g-C3N4. The orbit diagrams of VBM and conduction band minimum (CBM) of CN and GQD/CN show the band gap change. Figs. 1c and 2b show that the VBM of CN is composed of the N 2p orbitals. However, Figs. 1e and 2e show that the VBM of GQDs/CN is dominated by the C 2p orbitals in GQDs. The CBM keeps unchanged before and after the adsorption of GQDs (see Fig. 1d and f), which is dominated by C and N atoms in g-C3N4 (see Fig. 2b and e). Therefore, the VBM is located on GQDs while the CBM is located on g-C3N4 in GQDs/CN, which indicates that e
- excited from GQDs transport to g-C3N4 and the formation of type-Ⅱ heterojunction within GQDs/CN.

Fig. S5a shows the XRD patterns of CN and GQDs/CN nanomaterials. All samples have a strong diffraction peak and a weak peak at 13.0° and 27.6°, respectively. This result confirms the formation of a graphite-like stacking g-C3N4 layer. The peak at 13.0° is ascribed to the (1 0 0) facet of g-C3N4, which belongs to conjugated aromatic units of g-C3N4 [31]. The wide peak at 27.6° indicates the presence of (0 0 2) crystal plane, which corresponds to the in-plane tri-s-triazine ring [32]. In addition, the characteristic diffraction peak of GQDs does not appear on the patterns of GQDs/CN composites. It means that the introduction of GQDs did not lead to the development of new crystal orientations. This is likely due to the low loading of GQDs and good dispersion of GQDs into the composite materials [33].

To identify the existence of GQDs in the composite particles, the chemical composition and bonding configuration of CN and 9GQDs/CN were characterized by XPS analysis. Fig. S5b and Fig. 3 a–c shows the XPS results, where all spectra were calibrated by C 1s of aliphatic carbon at 284.8 eV. Fig. S5b shows the full survey spectra of GQDs, CN, and 9GQDs/CN materials, which illustrates the coexistence of C, O, and N elements in CN and 9GQDs/CN and C, Na, and O elements in GQDs. Na KLL Auger spectral peaks in GQDs spectrum may be derived from the residual Na in GQDs. The weak signal of O element in CN is attributed to the adsorption of oxygen from the atmosphere during preparation [34]. Fig. 3a illustrates the high-resolution spectra of C 1s. For the CN sample, the peaks at 284.76 eV, 285.84 eV, and 288.23 eV are attributed to the standard reference sp2 C–C bonds, sp3 C in C–NC, and sp2 C in N–CN, respectively [35,36]. The introduction of GQDs in g-C3N4 lowered the binding energies of C–NC bond and N–CN bond in the 9GQDs/CN samples. This slight shift is due to the difference in electron density caused by charge transfer between GQDs and g-C3N4 [37]. Fig. 3b shows the high-resolution O 1s spectra. The binding energy at 531.91 eV is assigned to the O–C/O–H bonds in GQDs [38], which indicates the loading of GQDs onto g-C3N4. In Fig. 3c, both the high-resolution N 1s spectra of CN and 9GQDs/CN reveal four fitted peaks. For the CN sample, the dominant peak at 398.78 eV is attributed to the CN–C bond (sp2 hybridization of N atom) in the s-triazine ring [39]. The peaks at 399.68 eV and 400.81 eV correspond to N-(C)3 and N–H bond (-NH2), respectively [35,40]. The weak peak at 404.73 eV is attributed to the π–π∗ excitations between the stacking interlayers [35]. The high-resolution N 1s spectra of 9GQDs/CN slightly shift to lower binding energies as C 1s spectra do, which is also originated from the electronic interaction between GQDs and g-C3N4 [41]. In summary, the high-resolution spectra of C 1s, O 1s, and N 1s all indicate the existence of GQDs in GQDs/CN samples. The charge transfer between GQDs and g-C3N4 resulting in the shift of C 1s and N 1s spectra is also confirmed by the DFT calculations in Section 3.1.

Fig. 3d–f and Fig. S6a show the morphologies of the prepared samples characterized by FE-SEM technique. The FE-SEM image of CN in Fig. S6a shows the lamellar structure of CN with layers stacked together. Fig. 3d–f presents the SEM images of 9GQDs/CN. The composite does not possess any layered structure; instead, rod-like shapes appear after combing GQDs with g-C3N4. The horizontal growth of g-C3N4 crystal is inhibited by the GQDs, and GQDs/CN tends to grow vertically. In addition, these nanorods intersect with each other forming a porous framework, which prolongs the residence time of gas on catalyst surface and allows the catalyst to degrade NO gas more efficiently.

Fig. 3g and Fig. S6b display the TEM images of 9GQDs/CN and CN, respectively, at the same magnitude of magnification. The TEM test is used to confirm that GQDs are successfully coupled to g-C3N4 in GQDs/CN samples. GQDs are not detected on the surface of CN nanosheets in Fig. S6b, but Fig. 3g shows that GQDs are evenly distributed on the surface of 9GQDs/CN. These results further prove the presence of GQDs in the GQDs/CN composite catalyst. Fig. 3h–j shows the HR-TEM images of the purified GQDs. Fig. 3h show the size of dot observed is less than 10 nm, indicating successful preparation of GQDs. Fig. 3i and j are the further magnification of A and B marked in the red circle in Fig. 3h, respectively. The measured lattice interplanar spacing of 0.21 nm (Figs. 3i) and 0.32 nm (Fig. 3j) correspond to the crystallographic (1 0 0) and (0 0 2) planes of GQDs [42,43], respectively.

Fig. S7a shows the N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm of CN and 9GQDs/CN. Both are type-IV isotherms and indicate the existence of mesopores [44]. The specific surface area and pore volume of 9GQDs/CN are 51.61 m2/g and 0.18 cm3/g, greater than 12.46 m2/g and 0.055 cm3/g of CN, respectively. This is because GQDs inhibit the overlap of g-C3N4 nanosheets (see Fig. S6a), and the vertical growth of crystals is conducive to the formation of a larger specific surface area. The results indicate more active sites on the surface of 9GQDs/CN and shortened transfer distance of active species [45]. These properties help improve the photocatalytic activity of the composite catalyst.

Fig. S7b shows the UV–Vis DRS spectra of CN and GQDs/CN composites. All samples present evident absorption from ultraviolet region to visible light below 550 nm. The ability of nanocomposites to absorb visible light is greatly enhanced by GQDs compared with CN. There are two reasons for such an improvement in light absorption. First, GQDs are electron media [46], which can promote the separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs and enhance light absorption. Second, sample color changes from pale yellow of CN to grayish yellow of GQDs/CN after adding GQDs. Such a change in color, as illustrated in the inset of Fig. S7b, also increases the light absorption [47]. The band gaps of the synthesized photocatalysts shown in Fig. 4a are determined by Kubelka-Munk (K-M) method [48] using Eq. (5):
(5)
F
(
R ∞
)
=
( 1 −
R ∞
)
2
2
R ∞
=
K ∗
s
where
R ∞
, K∗, and s are the diffuse reflectance, effective absorption co-efficient of the sample, and scattering coefficient, respectively. The band gaps are obtained via the equivalent absorption K-M units of the absorption edges, (F(R
∞ )hν)1/2 vs. hν, where h is Plank’ constant and ν is the light frequency. The band gaps of CN, 1GQDs/CN, 9GQDs/CN, and 15GQDs/CN are 2.64, 2.62, 2.60, and 2.58 eV, respectively. The preceding DRS results indicate that the addition of GQDs narrows the band gap of the prepared photocatalysts. This concurs the DFT calculation in Section 3.1.

Fig. 4b shows the XPS VB spectra of CN and 9GQDs/CN. The VB potentials of CN and 9GQDs/CN are 1.97 V and 1.94 V, respectively. Based on the work function of the XPS instrument, which is 4.55 eV, the calculated VB potentials of CN and 9GQDs/CN relative to the vacuum energy level are 6.52 V and 6.49 V, respectively. Then the VB potentials of CN and 9GQDs/CN relative to normal hydrogen electrode (NHE) can be calculated as 2.08 V and 2.05 V, respectively, because 4.44 eV against vacuum is equivalent to 0 V vs. NHE [49].

Fig. 4c shows the MS curves for CN and 9GQDs/CN. The positive slope indicates that the as-prepared CN and 9GQDs/CN are n-type semiconductors [50]. In addition, 9GQDs/CN has smaller MS plot slopes than CN does, indicating that 9GQDs/CN has a higher carrier density than that of CN [51]. The flat band potentials (E
fb ) for CN and 9GQDs/CN, located at −0.69 V and −0.67 V vs. SCE, are calculated using the Equation: E
(NHE)
= E
(SCE)
+ 0.24 V [52] to be −0.45 V and −0.43 V vs. NHE, respectively. Based on the MS test results, the CB potentials of CN and 9GQDs/CN are −0.55 V and −0.53 V vs. NHE, respectively, using another Equation (E
CB
= E
fb
– 0.10 V) [52]. Therefore, the band gap potentials (E
g ) of CN and 9GQDs/CN calculated using E
g = E
VB – E
CB are 2.63 eV and 2.58 eV, respectively. These two E
g are very close to the UV–Vis DRS test result in Fig. 4a (2.64 eV and 2.60 eV for CN and 9GQDs/CN, respectively). The excellent agreement between measured band gaps using UV–Vis DRS and calculated ones based on XPS VB and MS test indicates reliable band structures determined in this work. Furthermore, the MS results are used for further analysis and discussion in the following sections.

DFT calculations in Section 3.1 reveal that the CBM of GQDs/CN is located on g-C3N4. This well explains why the CB potentials of CN and 9GQDs/CN are close to each other. This agreement between DFT theory and experimental measurements including XPS VB spectra and MS results in Fig. 4 reiterates their reliability.

Fig. 4d illustrates the PL spectra of the CN and 9GQDs/CN. The CN exhibits strong PL intensity, which indicates the rapid recombination between photogenerated e
- and h
+ . In contrast, the PL intensity of 9GQDs photocatalysts dramatically declines, indicating that the presence of GQDs promotes the separation of e
- from h
+ in the composite catalyst. This finding is consistent with the transfer of photogenerated e
- from GQDs to g-C3N4 verified by DFT calculations (see Section 3.1).

The inset in the top left corner of Fig. 4d shows that the GQDs suspension is orange under natural condition (A), but it shows blue fluorescence under ultraviolet light (B). This corresponds to the position of maximum emission of GQDs at 462 nm [53]. It is noted that the maximum emissions of 9GQDs/CN and CN lie at 465 nm and 480 nm, respectively. Such a shift to the left of PL spectra peak may result from the excellent upconversion luminescence properties of GQDs [54].

Fig. 4e shows the EIS plots of the prepared catalysts. EIS test evaluates the separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs within a catalyst. In the Nyquist diagram, a smaller radius of the arc means a lower resistance of charge transfer [55]. The inset in Fig. 4e is the equivalent circuit diagram, where Rs and Rp are the electrolyte resistance and the interface charge transfer resistance, respectively [49]. The table inserted into Fig. 4e shows that the values of Rp of GQDs/CN samples with GQDs introduced are smaller than that of CN, which indicates GQDs/CN samples have smaller impedance than CN does. This is because GQDs provides a new path of transfer of photogenerated e
- through the interface of GQDs and g-C3N4. Moreover, the Nyquist-plot diameter of 9GQDs/CN is the smallest, which explains why 9GQDs/CN has the best activity for NO degradation.

The photocurrent measurement is used to further study the separation efficiency of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. Fig. 4f shows the photocurrent response plots of CN and GQDs/CN samples. The photocurrent responses of all GQDs/CN samples are enhanced compared to CN; this result is consistent with the EIS test results. The reason is that the introduction of GQDs allows photogenerated e
− and h
+ to accumulate on g-C3N4 and GQDs, respectively. Again it is confirmed by DFT simulation in Section 3.1. Fig. 4f shows that GQDs doping has major impact on photocurrents, indicating the importance of a proper loading of GQDs to photocatalysis of GQDs/CN composites. In this work, 9 mL of GQDs solution, which corresponds to the 9GQDs/CN nanocomposites, is deemed as the optimum option.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic activities of CN and GQDs/CN
ce:section.ce:para:
The time-series concentrations of NO and NO2 are presented in Fig. S8, which is then utilized to calculate the
η
,
S
N
O x −
and DeNO
x index according to Eqs. (1)–(3). Fig. 5 a shows the photocatalytic NO conversion rates of different catalysts during 1 h. The composite catalysts with GQDs have stronger degradation ability than CN does. Fig. S8a shows that all the GQDs/CN hybrids can degrade NO more effectively than CN does in 1 h under the visible light irradiation. There are three reasons for this performance improvement. First, GQDs/CN samples have porous framework, increasing the contact time of NO gas with the catalysts. Second, GQDs/CN samples have larger specific surface areas, providing more active sites. Third, GQDs can effectively transfer photogenerated e
- , inhibiting the recombination of e
- and h
+ . The 9GQDs/CN composite, which shows the best performance, indicates appropriate incorporation of GQDs to g-C3N4 is essential to NO photocatalysis. This finding is consistent with the EIS and photocurrent response test results in Section 3.4.2.

In addition, Table S3 compares the performances of our 9GQDs/CN with other catalysts in the literature for the degradation of NO. Gas hourly space velocity (GHSV), the ratio of feed gas flow rate to reactor volume, can determines the fluid-dynamic patterns and the species in the gas toward the catalytic layer. So GHSV is an important parameter for the evaluation of catalytic activity. The larger the value of GHSV, the greater the challenge for the catalyst to degrade NO [56]. It can be seen from Table S3 that the inlet NO concentration and GHSV are much higher, and the catalyst loading is lower in our current work. Even so, the as-prepared 9GQDs/CN catalyst are more effective in NO treatment than its competitors for a longer reaction time.

Fig. 5b and c shows the
S
N
O x −
and DeNOx index of different samples. A high selectivity of the formation of NOx − and a positive DeNOx index are preferred because they indicate little secondary pollution from the NO degradation. All GQDs/CN composites outweigh CN in terms of the two indicators. Again, 9GQDs/CN performs best. Specifically, the selectivity of NOx − rises from 53% of CN to 74% of 9GQDs/CN, and the DeNOx index increases from −0.25 of CN to 0.19 of 9GQDs/CN. This finding indicates that GQDs/CN has sufficient adsorption sites for NOx − generated by photocatalysis.

Fig. 5d shows five consecutive cycles of photocatalytic degradation over 9GQDs/CN catalyst. IC test results indicate that NO3 − is the predominant non-gas product during NO photocatalytic degradation in this work. The accumulation of NO3 − will occupy the active sites on the surface of the catalyst. A thermal treatment is then applied to regenerate the catalyst by removing the NO3 − ions from the catalyst surface (see Section 2.4). The photocatalytic NO conversion rate of 9GQDs/CN maintains 87% for 1 h after 5 cycles. This indicates the stability of the prepared GQDs/CN samples for photocatalytic degradation of NO.

The fate of nitrogen (N) element is key to unveiling the mechanism of NO photocatalysis. We choose NO photodegradation using 9GQDs/CN under visible light irradiation (λ > 380 nm) as an example to elaborate on the analysis of final products. Fig. S9 shows the time-series concentration curves of NO and NO2 at the outlet for the selected cases. The summation of the N-element in NO and NO2 at the outlet is unequal to the input N-element at the inlet; this result indicates that a large portion of the NO is converted into other nitrogen-containing species.

The primary non-gaseous products of photocatalytic degradation of NO are NOx − (x = 2 and/or 3). The IC test is used to determine the value of x and to quantify the amount of NOx − generated. Table S4 tabulates the results of IC test. Almost all generated NOx-ions are in the form of NO3 −, and the amount of NO2 − is negligible.

Table 1 summarizes the distribution of N-elements of photocatalytic degradation of NO. The amount of NO input is calculated using Eq. S(1) based on the NO concentration at the inlet. The amount of unremoved NO and the produced NO2 are calculated based on the time-series concentration curves at the outlet using Eqs. S2 and S3, respectively. The amount of NO3 − ions calculated from the IC result using Eq. S(5) equals that derived from the concentration measurements of gaseous unremoved NO and produced NO2 using Eq. S(4). The mass balance of N-element before and after photocatalysis confirms that the NO entering the photoreactor is converted into unremoved NO, the produced NO2, and the NO3 − adsorbed onto the catalysts surface.

Fig. S10 shows the distribution of N-element products and the fate of input NO. Based on the selected example, the products of NO photocatalysis using prepared samples are expected to be NO2 and NO3 −. Fig. 5e shows the distribution of N-element products in the photocatalytic degradation of NO using CN, 1GQDs/CN, 9GQDs/CN, and 15GQDs/CN, respectively, for Fig. S8.

The 9GQDs/CN composite is chosen as optimal photocatalyst in Sections 3.4 and 3.5 and therefore selected in the active species detection experiment. Fig. 5f shows the results of the scavenging experiment. The catalyst almost loses its ability to degrade NO with the addition of KI or K2Cr2O7, which indicates the essential role of h
+ and e
- in photocatalysis. When O2 or H2O are removed from the mixed gas, the performance of catalyst is also impaired. However, the impact of O2 on the performance of the catalyst is much greater than that of H2O, which suggests that
·
O 2 −
plays a more important role than
· O H
does in NO photocatalysis [57]. The above results will be verified again in the ESR test.

To further investigate the mechanism of photocatalytic degradation of NO using GQDs/CN, ESR spectroscopy is used to detect
·
O 2 −
and ·OH radicals in the GQDs, CN, and 9GQD/CN under both dark environment and visible light irradiation. Fig. 6 a and b shows the ESR results. The characteristic peaks of DMPO-·OH and DMPO-
·
O 2 −
can be detected when the samples are irradiated with visible light. On the contrary, both peaks disappear under dark conditions. These results indicate that ·OH and
·
O 2 −
are photoproducts in the photocatalytic degradation of NO. The
·
O 2 −
radicals are formed by the reduction reaction between adsorbed oxygen and photogenerated e
- . The ·OH species are derived from the oxidation of adsorbed H2O by photogenerated h
+ and from the hydroxylation reaction of
·
O 2 −
[27]. That partial ·OH radicals come from
·
O 2 −
also explains why
·
O 2 −
has a greater effect on NO photocatalysis than ·OH does in the active species test experiment. As compared to GQDs and CN, 9GQDs/CN has stronger signals of both DMPO-·OH and DMPO-
·
O 2 −
, which indicates that 9GQDs/CN can produce more ·OH and
·
O 2 −
when degrading NO. Based on the electrochemical test results (Fig. 4f), this is attributed to the that 9GQDs/CN can generate more photogenerated e
− and h
+ .

In-situ DRIFTS tests were used to explore the intermediate products of the catalyst in the process of NO adsorption and photocatalysis. Fig. 6c and d shows in-situ DRIFTS spectra of adsorption and NO photocatalysis of CN and 9GQDs/CN, respectively. The peak positions of CN and 9GQDs/CN are approximately the same. Table 2 summarizes the assignment of the peaks in the spectra. The spectra of NO adsorption at 30 min under dark conditions has no change compared with that at 20 min, which shows that NO adsorption has reached equilibrium. At the dark adsorption stage, the peaks of NO (1349 and 1348 cm−1) are attributed to the physical adsorption of NO molecules on the sample surface. The peaks of NO2 (1213 and 1217 cm−1) are derived from the oxidation of partial NO by O2. The peaks of NO2 − and NO3 − (1285, 1287, 815, and 890 cm−1) may result from the oxidation of reactive oxygen species formed by the activation of H2O (g) and O2 at the interface [58].

After turning on the Xe lamp, the new positions of NO2 (1080 and 1085 cm−1), NO2 −, and NO3 − (1130-1160 cm−1) peaks appear in the spectra of CN and 9GQDs/CN. The peak intensities of NO3 −, NO2, and NO2 − are significantly enhanced, especially in the 9GQDs/CN spectra in comparison with that in dark condition. This is because after the sample is excited by visible light, the generated h
+ ,
· O H
, and
·
O 2 −
can directly oxidize NO to NO3 −, NO2, and NO2 − [59]. The IC tests results show that all generated NOx − ions are in the form of NO3 −, so NO2 − is an intermediate of the photocatalysis of NO, which will be further oxidized by
· O H
to NO3 − following Eqs. (14) and (15).

Next comes further investigation of the mechanisms of NO photocatalysis based on redox potentials. The CB potential of GQDs (−0.58 V) in our previous research [20] is more negative than that of g-C3N4 (−0.55 V). In addition, GQDs is a visible-light responsive electron carrier [62]. The e
- excited onto the CB of GQDs can be transferred to the CB of g-C3N4, reducing the recombination of photogenerated e
- and h
+ .

The DFT simulation also shows that the VB of GQDs/CN is located on GQDs. XPS VB measurements show that the VB potential of GQDs/CN is 2.05 V. Thus, the VB potential of GQDs in GQDs/CN compound is 2.05 V. The VB potential of g-C3N4 determined by XPS VB analysis in Section 3.4.1 (2.08 V) is more positive than that of GQDs [63]. Therefore, h
+ left on the VB of g-C3N4 after electrons are excited by visible light can transport to the VB of GQDs. The preceding characteristics of e
- and h
+ transfer path indicates the formation of type-Ⅱ heterojunction within GQDs/CN nanocomposites. This mechanism is different from the conclusions drawn from other studies on the treatment of organics using GQDs/g-C3N4 [46,64], where GQDs only acted as an electron medium, and did not contribute to the formation of heterojunction.

The mechanism of NO oxidation by GQDs/CN can be explained by oxidation-reduction potential. The CB potential of 9GQDs/CN (−0.53 V vs. NHE) is sufficient for its e
- to reduce O2 to
⋅
O 2 −
(
O 2
/
⋅
O 2 −
, −0.33 V vs. NHE) [65] following Eq. (8); the VB potential of 9GQDs/CN (2.05 V vs. NHE) is also sufficient for h
+ to oxidize NO into HNO3 [66] and to oxidize OH− into
⋅ O H
(OH
− /
⋅ O H
, 1.90 V vs. NHE) [65] following Eqs. (7) and (12), respectively. Considering the DFT calculations, active species detection experiments, ESR tests, XPS VB spectra, and MS test, the mechanism of NO removal by GQDs/CN can be described using Eqs. (6–16).

Fig. 7 shows how we explore the mechanism of NO degradation by GQDs/CN via theoretical calculations, experimental investigations, and multiple characterizations. The schematic diagram of the mechanism behind NO photocatalysis using 9GQDs/CN catalysts is also illustrated in Fig. 8 .

GQDs/CN nanocomposites are proposed to efficiently photodegrade gaseous NO under visible-light irradiation with the guidance of DFT calculations. GQDs and CN are prepared by pyrolysis of CA and melamine, respectively. Then GQDs/CN heterojunction catalysts are hydrothermally synthesized. After introducing GQDs to g-C3N4, the specific surface area and pore volume of 9GQDs/CN composite increase by 4.15 and 3.27 times, respectively. They provide more adsorption and active sites for photocatalytic abatement of NO. Compared with CN, the optimal 9GQDs/CN hybrids increase the conversion rate of NO from 61% to 90%, the selectivity of nitrate formation from 53% to 74%, and the DeNO
x index from −0.25 to 0.19. Active species detection experiment and ESR test show that e
- , h
+ , and
⋅
O 2 −
are the main active substances in the photocatalytic degradation of NO, while the impact of ·OH is negligible. In-situ DRIFTS tests identify NO2 − as an intermediate product of NO photocatalysis. XPS VB analysis, MS tests, UV–Vis DRS, and DFT calculations all verify the formation of type-Ⅱ heterojunction between GQDs and g-C3N4. The cyclic experiment shows that 9GQDs/CN exhibits stability in the degradation of NO, which is important to industrial applications.
